questions.prompt: Quick gut check: when is the implication `P implies Q` false?

answers.text: I'd go with this: In classical logic an implication with a false premise is true.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. In classical logic an implication with a false premise is true.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. In classical logic an implication with a false premise is true.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: it is equivalent to `not P or Q`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It is equivalent to `not P or Q`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It is equivalent to `not P or Q`.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, when P is true and Q is false.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. When P is true and Q is false.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. When P is true and Q is false.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: what does the union of two sets contain?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Duplicate membership does not create duplicate set elements.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Duplicate membership does not create duplicate set elements.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Duplicate membership does not create duplicate set elements.

answers.text: From what I've seen, every element that is in either set or both.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Every element that is in either set or both.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Every element that is in either set or both.

answers.text: The practical answer is: only elements common to both sets.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Common elements form the intersection.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Common elements form the intersection.

questions.prompt: I've seen mixed takes, so: what is true of a finite undirected tree with n greater than or equal to 1 vertices?

answers.text: I think the important part is this: it has n minus 1 edges.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It has n minus 1 edges.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It has n minus 1 edges.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, it contains at least one cycle.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A tree is connected and acyclic.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A tree is connected and acyclic.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Every vertex in a tree has degree at least two.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Trees with more than one vertex have leaves of degree one, and a one-vertex tree has degree zero.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Trees with more than one vertex have leaves of degree one, and a one-vertex tree has degree zero.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: what is the negation of `P and Q`?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: De Morgan's law changes AND to OR when negating the expression.

feedback.sus: This one is legit. The law has an analogous set-complement form.

answers.text: The practical answer is: `not P or not Q`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. `not P or not Q`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. `not P or not Q`.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: what makes a function injective?

answers.text: The way I understand it: each output has at most one preimage.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Each output has at most one preimage.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Each output has at most one preimage.

answers.text: Short version: Different inputs never map to the same output.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Different inputs never map to the same output.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Different inputs never map to the same output.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Every element of the codomain must be reached.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Reaching the whole codomain defines surjectivity.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Reaching the whole codomain defines surjectivity.

questions.prompt: No searching: what makes a function surjective?

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Every element of the codomain has at least one preimage.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Every element of the codomain has at least one preimage.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Every element of the codomain has at least one preimage.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Every input maps to a different output.

feedback.legit: Not this one. That condition describes injectivity.
feedback.sus: Good catch. That condition describes injectivity.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Surjectivity prevents two different inputs from sharing an output.

feedback.legit: Not this one. That restriction is injectivity; a surjective function may map multiple inputs to one output.
feedback.sus: Good catch. That restriction is injectivity; a surjective function may map multiple inputs to one output.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: when does order matter: permutations or combinations?

answers.text: Short version: Order matters only for combinations.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Combinations select groups without order.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Combinations select groups without order.

answers.text: I'd go with this: Choosing a president and vice president is a permutation-style assignment.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Choosing a president and vice president is a permutation-style assignment.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Choosing a president and vice president is a permutation-style assignment.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: order matters for permutations.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Order matters for permutations.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Order matters for permutations.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what are the core steps for proving a statement over consecutive integers by induction?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Strong induction may assume all earlier cases in the inductive step.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Strong induction may assume all earlier cases in the inductive step.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Strong induction may assume all earlier cases in the inductive step.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Prove a base case and prove that one case implies the next.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Prove a base case and prove that one case implies the next.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Prove a base case and prove that one case implies the next.

answers.text: From what I've seen, verify a large sample and assume the rest.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Induction is a proof for all cases in the domain, not sampling.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Induction is a proof for all cases in the domain, not sampling.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: what does the pigeonhole principle guarantee?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: If more objects than boxes are assigned, some box receives at least two objects.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. If more objects than boxes are assigned, some box receives at least two objects.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. If more objects than boxes are assigned, some box receives at least two objects.

answers.text: From what I've seen, every box receives exactly the same number of objects.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The principle guarantees a collision, not equal distribution.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The principle guarantees a collision, not equal distribution.

answers.text: The practical answer is: the pigeonhole principle identifies exactly which box must contain the collision.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The principle guarantees that a collision exists without identifying a particular box.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The principle guarantees that a collision exists without identifying a particular box.

questions.prompt: I've seen mixed takes, so: for a positive integer n, what does `a` congruent to `b` modulo `n` mean?

feedback.sus: This one is legit. `n` divides `a - b`.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: what does Bayes' theorem relate?

answers.text: Short version: Bayes' theorem requires the two events to be independent.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Bayes' theorem is useful precisely for relating conditional probabilities and does not require independence.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Bayes' theorem is useful precisely for relating conditional probabilities and does not require independence.

answers.text: I'd go with this: It allows base rates to be ignored.

feedback.sus: Good catch. The prior probabilities are essential terms in Bayes' theorem.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: Bayes' theorem says `P(A given B)` always equals `P(B given A)`.
